dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 respond to a traffic incident with potential injuries at the intersection of Broad and Adams.
Hi there, I'm Eli with EMS. I understand you're having some pain after the accident. My partner here is Naomi. Can we start by asking what brought you to call 911 today?
My friend here, Marcus, had a bad reaction to the motorcycle accident. It looks like he hurt his right knee badly, and he won't let anyone move it now.
Yeah, my right knee hit pavement, man. Worst part is when anyone tries to move me. It's an 8/10, no lie.
Marcus, can you follow my voice? Can you tell me your name?
Marcus, y'all. Yeah, I'm aware.
I'm going to ask a few more questions to assess your alertness. Do you know what city we're in?
[CITY].
What happened to bring you to the hospital today?
I was riding my bike on the main lanes, and a car pulled out from the side road. I tried to avoid it, but laid the bike down anyway. Hardest hit was my right leg.
Can you squeeze my fingers as I hold this up to check your movement? Left, okay, now right-yes, I see. You seem to be moving both sides. Are you having trouble with your body anywhere else?
Back pain's bad, like centered along my spine, no radiating. Lemme try not to move more than needed.
We'll be careful with your placement. Before we do any more movement, let's look at your airway. I've got a manual c-spine hold to keep your neck stable. Can you speak with ease?
Yes, I can speak.
Marcus, I'm going to check your breathing now. Are you having difficulty taking a deep breath or notice any pain when breathing deeply?
Breathing's okay.
Let me count your breathing. That's 24 per minute.
Marcus, can I take your pulse? I'll be gentle. That was a 126, fast but strong. Your blood pressure is 145/72. Please let us know if anything starts making you feel too cold or clammy.
Your pulse seems stable at the moment.
Alright, blood pressure is 145 over 72.
Skin looks normal, warm and dry, but I can see some abrasions. Are they associated with the pain?
Tell me about any other injuries you recall besides the right knee. You mentioned some scraping and road rash.
Yeah, my left side-arm, elbow, flank-looks scraped too. But I'm not throwing up or losing consciousness.
Are you currently taking any medications for that or other purposes?
Yeah, I take Adderall, mostly for ADHD.
You've been diagnosed for ADHD? Any other medical problems in the past?
No, that's about it. I had surgery on my left wrist before.
On a scale of 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst, how would you rate your pain right now?
8/10 when still. 10 or worse if I do move it.
Does the pain increase when you try to take a deep breath or move your leg? Does it radiate elsewhere?
Worse when moving. Doesn't travel elsewhere-just stays in the one spot.
Let me look closely at the skin injuries you have. I can see some abrasions and road rash on the left side. These look controlled. Any signs of other injuries?
Yeah, his hands were scraped but probably due to gloves. His legs look like heavily scraped-on left and right-but the right knee has a pronounced, closed deformity.
Naomi, can you help me keep the patient's neck manually stabilized while I remove his helmet-since a C-collar is in place?
Let me carefully examine your legs and back. That looks like a pronounced deformity in your right lower leg and knee. Your back pain is along your spine.
I'm going to be checking your blood sugar now, Marcus. You might feel a slight prick. Let's go: 108 mg/dL.
Also, your oxygen level is at 100% with your current breathing.
I'm going to do a quick EKG now to monitor your heart. Stay still, please.
The rhythm on the monitor appears to be regular. We're seeing sinus tachycardia, likely from your pain and stress.
I successfully placed an 18-gauge IV in your left arm, Marcus, for any medications or fluids needed.
Your heart beats are currently 107; rhythm is fast but detectable.
Your blood pressure is now 127/71.
After a cross-check, we are giving you 100 micrograms of Fentanyl to manage your pain.
Oxygen saturation has slightly dropped to 98%, but it's within acceptable range.
We're placing the patient on a backboard now, full spinal immobilization in line with protocol.
We are starting trauma management under general trauma protocols for lower leg injuries and possible spinal trauma from the crash.
We've placed you on a backboard with full spinal immobilization following protocol.
We are also applying 4-lead ECG monitoring to track your heart during transport. Just remain still while we do this.
You are alert and oriented x4 (GCS 15) with no signs of confusion, so we'll maintain your current positioning.
I'm going to secure you gently to the backboard, ensuring the right leg is in a position of comfort. You okay with that, Marcus?
Yeah, just be gentle. Otherwise, I won't be answering anymore.
Your field impression is consistent with lower leg injury and possible epidural hemorrhage from trauma. Trauma protocols in use.
My name is Naomi, helping move you now. We'll secure you and prevent further movement or pain during transport.
We've contacted [CITY], and Trauma 3 is ready. You have a trauma alert ahead, Marcus.
You're at 119 now, heart is still fast but very detectable.
Your BP is holding steady at 120/60.
99% oxygen. We're monitoring closely for any changes.
Pain level was 8 before Fentanyl, but after administration, you reported 4/10. Good job so far.
With new assessments, your current pain in the leg is down and now rates at 4/10. Any new pain or changes since the medication?
Pain is lower, feels more like a bore than a wound on my leg.
You are now safely positioned on the stretcher. As we secure you, we remain still to avoid worsening pain.
We've placed you on a secured stretcher and will be monitoring throughout transport.
Lastly, we confirm you are alert and aware of your surroundings-person, place, time, and event. That is AOx4.
Ride is starting shortly. As we move, we'll continue monitoring and provide any needed interventions.
We confirm the patient is alert and oriented x4 with GCS 15. All interventions and medications have been properly recorded.
